Not sure I understand your question correctly. The specialized lookup used
in IntOptionParameter and FlagParameter is implemented in NamedParameter
IIRC and only concerns short options. The general lookup is done in the
parser loop (it could perhaps be moved to a method of CliSignature) and
doesn't care about the parameter's type as long as it is in sig.aliases.

@meshy I use pipenv, yet I think this PR is handy. For the following reasons:

1. `pipenv --where` will only work when it can find a Pipfile in the current directory or parent directories. If not,

```
Courtesy Notice: Pipenv found itself running within a virtual environment, so it will automatically use that environment, instead of creating its own for any project.
No Pipfile present at project home. Consider running `pipenv install` first to automatically generate a Pipfile for you.
```
2. For some reason, I cannot filter out pipenv's `Courtesy notice: Pipenv found itself running within a virtual environment ...`, despite using something like:

```
~/s/s/pew ❯❯❯ pipenv --where | tail -n 1                                                                                                                                                   (sandbox) 
Courtesy Notice: Pipenv found itself running within a virtual environment, so it will automatically use that environment, instead of creating its own for any project.
/home/avmo/src/sandbox/pew
```

1. do you have enough active tasks waiting to be fetched?
2. the traffic control algo is [token bucket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Token_bucket), which means 1 second algo generate 10 tokens into bucket, but if the bucket is full, the tokens will be dropped. Scheduler have a scheduler period, generally less than 1 second, but somethings not, in that case, you will loss some token. increase the burst size to 3~5 * rate
3. on dashboard, you can find queue size between components, it will tell you where are the bottleneck.

@LuisSoares the easiest way to do this is to host the image somewhere else (S3, photobucket, etc.) and reference that image within the getCussomCSS method. Here's an example using a photo I uploaded to photobucket (here: http://s95.photobucket.com/user/adamjdanger/media/indonesia_bg.jpg.html) ```python from spyre import server class SimpleApp(server.App): title = "Simple App" inputs = [{ "type":"text", "key":"words", "label":"write words here", "value":"hello world", "action_id":"simple_html_output"}] outputs = [{"type":"html", "id":"simple_html_output"}] def getHTML(self, params): words = params["words"] return "Here's what you wrote in the textbox: %s" % words def getCustomCSS(self): css = """body { background-image: url("http://i95.photobucket.com/albums/l141/adamjdanger/indonesia_bg.jpg");}""" return css app = SimpleApp() app.launch() ``` If you wanted a completely self-contained app (that worked without internet access) you could create an endpoint for your image. Make sure the image you're using as the background is in the same directory you are launching your app from and add a `getImage` method to your app and an additional output of type `image`. Here's an example: ```python from spyre import server import matplotlib.image as mpimg class SimpleApp(server.App): title = "Simple App" inputs = [{ "type":"text", "key":"words", "label":"write words here", "value":"hello world", "action_id":"simple_html_output"}] outputs = [{"type":"html", "id":"simple_html_output"}, {"type" : "image", "id" : "bg_image", "on_page_load" : False}] def getHTML(self, params): words = params["words"] return "Here's what you wrote in the textbox: %s" % words def getCustomCSS(self): css = """body { background-image: url("image?output_id=bg_image");}""" return css def getImage(self, params): img = mpimg.imread('indonesia_bg.jpg') return img app = SimpleApp() app.launch() ``` A couple of things to note here. Make sure you import `matplotlib.image`. Also, make sure you set the attribute `on_page_load` to `False` for the image output (otherwise it will try to load as an output and your background). Finally the url you specify in the css is `image?output_id=bg_image` where "bg_image" was the output_id we specified in our output's attributes.

The man page for `openssl mime` says you can pass a raw, DER-encoded PKCS#7 structure using `openssl -inform der -in tmp`. This would at least rule out any encoding issues adding the problem, although my guess is that is unlikely.

It is certainly possible there is an error in one of the CMS structure definitions. I've used some via oscrypto because `.p12` files use PKCS#7 to encrypt key data and certificates, but I'm sure there are some variations that haven't been hit before.

What may be helpful in debugging is to pass a known good file into asn1crypto and use the `.debug()` function to dump the complete structure and all of the encoding details. Comparing this to what is being constructed in Python would probably be one of the fastest paths to figuring it out.

I wonder if the fix would be, in this case, to provide the parameters as CHAR instead of VARCHAR.  I'm very concerned about what problems that would cause when comparing to actual varchar columns.

This is related to a couple of other issues where we need to know the SQL type of the parameters according to the SQL, not the values we're providing.  For example, if we're passing the number '7' that is being used to compare against a bigint field, it would be better if we passed as bigint rather than int.  We don't know until we ask the driver, but that requires a compilation.

The current version of pyodbc always prepares SQL if parameters are provided, so it is possible that the performance would not change.  I was planning on eliminating the prepare, but we could use the flag to keep it.

How do you mean a typed list? A `pyangbind.lib.yangtypes.TypedList` item is just a list, so it can be iterated through using `for x in leaf_list`. A `YANGListType` list can be iterated through using `for item in yang_list`.

chart.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$C$1:$C$5'})

In general futures aren't intended handle things with callbacks. The callback mechanism is more for applying some processing/transformations to the response before your application handles them via the futures object. If the future object goes out of scope the handle to get at the response will be lost and as you've seen nothing will be blocking/waiting for things to finish. Generally you need to save up the futures objects and call `resp = future.result()` on them or `resp = yield future`, as appropriate, to get at the response. Those will block or wait until things are done.

Do you have the django-sesame middleware enabled?

If you do, then I don't think you need that code:

- `get_object` could simply return `self.request.user`
- you don't need to preserve the token in the URL; the logged in user is already preserved in the session

If you want to have different access depending on whether the user authenticated with a sesame token or with a login and password, I think you should listen to the `user_logged_in` signal and record in the session which backend was used for authenticating. Then your views can check this value in the session.

@lastboy 

So... I'm pretty sure I found the problem. I'm sorry for not realizing this earlier...

I took another look at the error that you happened to post:

```
botocore.vendored.requests.exceptions.ConnectionError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='email.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: / (Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to proxy.', OSError('Tunnel connection failed: 503 Service Unavailable',)))
```

So... that's throwing an error trying to connect to email.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com. [SES doesn't exist in that region](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/rande.html#ses_region). That also explains why your tests of connecting to EC2 succeeded. SES only exists in the US and EU regions.

There's already [code in the SES service class](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/commit/5f7024698fc924c84543ee58e51816d24f19cdf1) that handles this condition gracefully. However, it appears that having the proxy in the middle is hiding the actual error message from AWS - without the proxy this would result in botocore [detecting the problem](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/28b8831f5f8518bef5accf994fc377c58a3569f7/botocore/endpoint.py#L163) and throwing an [EndpointConnectionError](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/2def57f4f899ad749db8d750010c3712d854f703/botocore/exceptions.py#L63) (which awslimitchecker knows to ignore).

However, botocore is seeing a ConnectionError ``(Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to proxy.', OSError('Tunnel connection failed: 503 Service Unavailable',))``, which doesn't look like any error that it knows how to deal with, so botocore just raises it.

As far as I can tell, the problem here is that your proxy isn't returning the same response that AWS would. I'm not really sure what to suggest... aside from either fixing your proxy, or not calling awslimitchecker for services that don't exist in a given region.

I had the same problem, and have solved it. 
I'm using anaconda3 python3.5 and when setup.py generate install project, it get jpeg library from `%Anaconda3%/envs/%your_env%/Library/lib/jpeg.lib`
This jpeg.lib using by pyqt package. And there are functions, that don't agree with declspec needed by dlib. 

To solve problem, switch your cmd to your virtual_env, and upgrade libjpeg from anaconda channel, e.g.:
`activate my_env`
`conda install -c anaconda libjpeg`

After that, go to your `%Anaconda3%/envs/%your_env%/Library/lib/` directory and make sure, that you have exactly `libjpeg.lib`, then temprorary move files `jpeg.lib` and `jpeg-static.lib` to your home directory.

Now, your have true `libjpeg.lib`, that python's setup.py cmake catch up from `%Anaconda3%/envs/%your_env%/Library/lib/`.

Run `python setup.py install --yes USE_AVX_INSTRUCTIONS --yes DLIB_USE_CUDA` and enjoy.

Don't forget move back your 'jpeg.lib' to `%Anaconda3%/envs/%your_env%/Library/lib/` directory provide for pyqt package.

Note, that your don't using virtual env, your lib directory will be `%Anaconda3%/Library/lib/`

`ZstdCompressor.write_to()` can be used to wrap a file object with compression. (It may be renamed to `stream_writer()` in a future release.

That being said, supporting `tarfile` natively might be a bit... funky. The reason is that CPython's `tarfile` insists on doing a `seek(-1, os.SEEK_CUR)`. This is immediately followed by a `read(1)`. Why it does this, I'm not sure.

The latest commit on `master` does support `seek()`. But only if advancing: seeking to a previous offset is not supported.

I coded up a test for `tarfile` round tripping. Although it currently fails due to the need to seek in reverse on the read side of things (writing seems to work fine).

I agree that tar support is worth pursuing. Let me poke at things to see if there's a reasonable way to implement reading from zstd compressed tar files.

Are you sure? That happens below the Engine.IO level, right? I ran the `attachment_count` parsing snippet of the Socket.IO-level packet parsing code:

```python
>>> ep = "31[]"   # 4 is removed at the Engine.IO level as its type
>>> # Extracted from socketio/packet.py :: Packet.encode()
>>> ep = ep[1:]
>>> dash = (ep + '-').find('-')
>>> attachment_count = 0
>>> if ep[0:dash].isdigit():
...     attachment_count = int(ep[0:dash])
...     ep = ep[dash + 1:]
... 
>>> attachment_count
0
>>> ep
'1[]'
```

The problem I described wouldn't occur because `31[]` isn't loaded by the Engine.IO level as an `int`, and thus Socket.IO's parsing code would load 3 as the type and 1 as the ID (the 4 being removed as the type at the Engine.IO level). Whereas if it _was_ interpreted as an `int`, the indexing [on this line](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/blob/master/socketio/packet.py#L84) fails and means `self.packet_type = 31`, exactly what this bug outlines. :)

I have the same issue, so I create a patch to work with `pip install recommonmark`.

save this code to `patch.py`, and import patch in `conf.py`, after this `sphinx-markdown-tables` and `recommonmark` are all works correct.

You may remove build dir and rebuild all to see the output.

## patch.py

```
# coding: utf-8

from recommonmark.parser import CommonMarkParser, _SectionHandler, append_inlines
from CommonMark import DocParser
from docutils import nodes


def parse(self, inputstring, document):
    self._patch_id = 0
    self.setup_parse(inputstring, document)

    self.document = document
    self.current_node = document
    self.section_handler = _SectionHandler(document)

    parser = DocParser()

    ast = parser.parse(inputstring + '\n')

    self.convert_block(ast)

    self.finish_parse()


# Blocks
def section(self, block):
    self._patch_id += 1
    new_section = nodes.section()
    new_section.line = block.start_line
    new_section['level'] = block.level

    title_node = nodes.title()
    title_node.line = block.start_line
    append_inlines(title_node, block.inline_content)
    new_section.append(title_node)
    name = nodes.fully_normalize_name(title_node.astext())
    new_section['names'].append(name)
    self.current_node.document.note_implicit_target(new_section, new_section)
    # nodes.make_id(name)
    new_section['ids'].append('id' + str(self._patch_id))

    self.section_handler.add_new_section(new_section, block.level)
    self.current_node = new_section


CommonMarkParser.parse = parse
CommonMarkParser.section = section

```

## conf.py

```
import path.to.patch  # noqa

...other config
```

The PS node is a bit special because in TF, it just runs forever until you kill it.  For TFoS, we actually start it in a background process, and then run a simple listener in the foreground of the executor/worker.  The `TFCluster.shutdown` sends a msg to this listener, which is then responsible for killing the PS process... so if the Spark executor is shutdown before receiving a stop msg, it's a bit tricky.  In YARN, the PS task will be killed (by YARN) if the parent process (executor) dies unexpectedly.  But for other environments, we might need something like a user-configurable SIGTERM handler on the executor side.  Is there an easy way to do that in Spark?

Ah - okay, I should probably have described the problem better.

I have two functions - the first one produces a Query, like:

```
query = db.my_table.with_alias("my_aliased_table").some_field == "example"
```
...and passes this query on to another function, like:
```
result = other_function(query)
```

Then, that other_function performs a select from a left join that includes the same aliased table:
```
left = [db.my_table.with_alias("my_aliased_table").on(...join expression...), ...other join...]
rows = db(query & ...other_query).select(..., left=left)
```

Now, this raises a "Name Conflict" error - because there seem to be two tables "my_aliased_table".

Obviously, this is exactly the same table with exactly the same alias (so perfectly the same resulting SQL), but merge_tablemaps doesn't recognize that because table.with_alias() produces a new instance of the Table every time it is called, and merge_tablemaps checks for object identity.

As I see it, with_alias stores the aliased table instance in the DAL instance (self._db), so it should be able to detect when it is called again for the same table with the same alias, and in that case return the same instance from self._db.

I can work around the issue by wrapping table.with_alias in a function that does exactly that: checking whether db already has an aliased instance of the same table with the same alias - and in that case, return that instance rather than creating a new one with with_alias.

That wrapper function should though not be necessary - I think, Table.with_alias should do that, so that this would succeed (if you need a unit test):
```
assertTrue(table.with_alias("alias") is table.with_alias("alias"))
```

```python
import zmq.asyncio
import zmq
import asyncio
import os
import pickle
import concurrent.futures
import time
import logging
import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web
import tempfile
import multiprocessing
import traceback

class Terminator:
    pass

class SocketCtx:
    def __init__(self, socket_type, high_water_mark: int, socket_name: str, connect_method_name: str, zmq_ctx: zmq.asyncio.Context = None):
        self._zmq_ctx = zmq_ctx
        self._socket = None
        self._socket_type = socket_type
        self._socket_name = socket_name
        self._high_water_mark = high_water_mark
        self._connect_method_name = connect_method_name
        self._pending_terminator = None
        self._our_ctx = zmq_ctx is None

    async def __aenter__(self):
        assert not self._socket

        if self._our_ctx:
            self._zmq_ctx = zmq.asyncio.Context()

        self._socket = self._zmq_ctx.socket(self._socket_type)
        self._socket.RCVHWM = self._socket.SNDHWM = self._high_water_mark
        self._socket.LINGER = 20  # if the receiver dies/closes, we don't want to hang on socket.close
        self._socket.SNDTIMEO = 20  # if the receiver dies/closes, we don't want to hang on socket.send

        getattr(self._socket, self._connect_method_name)(self._socket_name)

        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):

        if self._socket and self._socket_type == zmq.PULL:
            # pull everything from our in_queue so we don't hang on socket.close()
            try:
                while True:
                    await self.recv_pyobj_noblock()
            except:
                pass

        if self._our_ctx:
            self._zmq_ctx.destroy()  # will close all sockets associated with context and linger until all data sent
            self._zmq_ctx = None
            self._socket = None
        elif self._socket:
            self._socket.close()
            self._socket = None

    async def send_terminator(self):
        return await self.send_pyobj(Terminator())

    async def recv(self, *args, **kwargs):
        result = await self._socket.recv(*args, **kwargs)
        return result

    async def recv_pyobj_noblock(self):
        # If self._pending_terminator is set, will send all pending messages in the socket's in-queue and
        # then send the terminator last.  Otherwise will raise zmq.error.Again if there are no more pending messages.
        try:
            data = await self.recv(zmq.NOBLOCK)
            pyobj = pickle.loads(data)
            return pyobj
        except zmq.error.Again:
            if self._pending_terminator:
                pyobj = self._pending_terminator
                self._pending_terminator = None
                return pyobj
            raise

    async def recv_pyobj(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if self._pending_terminator:
            return await self.recv_pyobj_noblock()

        data = await self.recv(*args, **kwargs)
        pyobj = pickle.loads(data)
        if pyobj.__class__ is Terminator:
            self._pending_terminator = pyobj
            return await self.recv_pyobj_noblock()

        return pyobj

    async def send(self, data):
        return await self._socket.send(data)

    async def send_pyobj(self, obj):
        pyobj = pickle.dumps(obj)
        return await self._socket.send(pyobj)


def sender(socket_name):
    loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

    async def doit():
        async with SocketCtx(zmq.PUSH, 2, socket_name, 'connect') as zmq_socket:
            # we send 20,000 values at a time
            data = {y: {x: 140 for x in range(100)} for y in range(200)}

            total_sent = 0
            try:
                for i in range(25):
                    await zmq_socket.send_pyobj(data)
                    total_sent += len(data)
            except Exception as e:
                print(e)

            print("total sent:", total_sent)
            return total_sent

    result = loop.run_until_complete(doit())
    return result


async def receiver(connected_event: asyncio.Event, socket_name):
    async with SocketCtx(zmq.PULL, 100, socket_name, 'bind') as zmq_socket:
        connected_event.set()

        total_received = 0

        while True:
            try:
                pyobj = await zmq_socket.recv_pyobj()
                if isinstance(pyobj, Terminator):
                    break

                await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
                total_received += len(pyobj)
            except:
                traceback.print_exc()

    print("total received:", total_received)
    return total_received


async def ooo_test(loop):
    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as temp_dir:
        socket_name = "ipc://" + temp_dir + "/foobar"
        connected_event = asyncio.Event()
        recvr_task = asyncio.ensure_future(receiver(connected_event, socket_name))
        await connected_event.wait()

        executor = concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor(1)
        total_sent = await loop.run_in_executor(executor, sender, socket_name)
        executor.shutdown()
        print("total sent:", total_sent)

        async with SocketCtx(zmq.PUSH, 1, socket_name, 'connect') as zmq_socket:
            await zmq_socket.send_pyobj(Terminator())

        await recvr_task


async def get_method(request: aiohttp.web.Request):
    app = request.app

    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as temp_dir:
        socket_name = "ipc://" + temp_dir + "/foobar"
        sender_task = receiver_task = None
        try:
            connected_event = asyncio.Event()
            receiver_task = asyncio.ensure_future(receiver(connected_event, socket_name), loop=app['loop'])
            await connected_event.wait()

            sender_task = app['loop'].run_in_executor(app['executor'], sender, socket_name)
            await sender_task
            sender_task = None

            async with SocketCtx(zmq.PUSH, 1, socket_name, 'connect') as zmq_socket:
                await zmq_socket.send_pyobj(Terminator())

            await receiver_task
            receiver_task = None
        except:
            traceback.print_exc()

            try:
                if sender_task:
                    sender_task.cancel()
                    await sender_task
            finally:
                if receiver_task:
                    receiver_task.cancel()
                    await receiver_task

    return aiohttp.web.json_response(dict())


async def do_shutdown(request: aiohttp.web.Request):
    # this is here for unittests
    request.app.loop.stop()
    return aiohttp.web.json_response(dict())


def http_server():
    logging.basicConfig()

    loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

    app = aiohttp.web.Application(loop=loop)
    app['executor'] = concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor(6)
    app['loop'] = loop

    app.router.add_route('GET', '/', get_method)
    app.router.add_route('GET', '/shutdown', do_shutdown)

    with app['executor']:
        aiohttp.web.run_app(app)


async def aiohttp_test(loop):
    server = multiprocessing.Process(target=http_server)
    server.start()

    await asyncio.sleep(2)

    session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
    await asyncio.gather(*[session.get('http://localhost:8080') for i in range(500)])
    await session.get('http://localhost:8080/shutdown')
    print('done')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    _loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(_loop)

    #_loop.run_until_complete(ooo_test(_loop))
    _loop.run_until_complete(aiohttp_test(_loop))

That's untrue. If subcommand didn't work, it wouldn't be how most Jupyter and IPython commands are implemented today. A subcommand is an alternate application that should be instantiated as the singleton, e.g. `jupyter notebook` or `ipython kernel` or `git diff`. There shouldn't ever be more than one subcommand instantiated in a single process, but if you want to do this (e.g. for testing), then clearing the instance makes sense. If a subcommand isn't the global instance, that would defeat the whole purpose of subcommands.

The code below works differently in ipython (terminal) and ipython notebook. In crafting this example, I learned a little more about the problem, which I think I could avoid if I better understood how to make an Application subclass that worked properly (and identically) when instantiated from both an ipython terminal and ipython notebook. With a trivial example like this it seems silly. But with a real Application that is responsible for gluing together several complex Configurable objects it is not so obvious (to me at least). Here is a simple example that demonstrates my original problem:

```python
from traitlets.config import Configurable, Application
from traitlets import Unicode, Int, Float, List

import sys

class MyClass(Configurable):
    str = Unicode(u'default string', config=True)
    an_int = Int(1, config=True)
    
    def __init__(self, log, **kwargs):
        super(MyClass, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self._log = log
        
    def debug(self, msg):
        self._log.debug(msg)
        
    def info(self, msg):
        self._log.info(msg)
                
    def warn(self, msg):
        self._log.warn(msg)
                
    def error(self, msg):
        self._log.error(msg)
        
    def test_log(self):
        self.debug('dbg')
        self.info('info')
        self.warn('warn')
        self.error('error')

class MyApp(Application):
    a_float = Float(1.23, config=True)
    
    classes = List([
        MyClass,
    ])

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(MyApp, self).__init__(**kwargs)
    
    
def main(argv=None):
    cls = MyClass(MyApp.instance().log)
    cls.test_log()
    
def main2(argv=None):
    cls = MyClass(MyApp().log)
    cls.test_log()

print('calling main2')    
main2(['hello'])
print('works with ipython but not notebook')

print('using logger from host application instance')
MyClass(Application.instance().log).test_log()
print('works with ipython but not notebook')

print('using a.test.logger')
import logging
# could also reload and reconfigure, but do not need too
#reload(logging)
#logging.basicConfig(format='%(asctime)s %(levelname)s:%(message)s', level=logging.DEBUG, datefmt='%I:%M:%S')
mylogger = logging.getLogger('a.test.logger')
mylogger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
MyClass(mylogger).test_log()
print('works with both')


#if __name__ == "__main__":
#   main2(sys.argv)
```

```python
def jwt_required(fn, realm=None):
    """
    Modified from original jwt_required to comply with `flask-rest-jsonapi` decorator conventions
    View decorator that requires a valid JWT token to be present in the request
    :param realm: an optional realm
    """
    @wraps(fn)
    def decorator(*args, **kwargs):
        _jwt_required(realm or app.config['JWT_DEFAULT_REALM'])
        return fn(*args, **kwargs)
    return decorator
```

S3 is an object store, not a file system. Your object has the key `param/file.txt`, but that key is an opaque string, there's no concept of a folders or directories. Some systems will attempt to use s3 like a file system by creating empty objects to represent folders. boto3 will not create or delete those placeholder objects unless you explicitly do so.

If you have other questions regarding s3's behavior I would recommend asking on stack overflow or contacting support.

Please see the sample at https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi-community-samples/blob/master/samples/export_vm.py

A key thing to note is that ExportVm does not return a task. It returns an HttpNfcLease. The lease gives you details for downloading the VM. And during that download, you need to continue to issue progress updates else the server will time out the lease (thinking that you abandoned it).

BTW I do this to prepend the virtualenv name to prompt during Clink startup. This is registered as a Clink prompt filter during startup.

```lua
local function find_virtualenv_prompt()
    local virtual_env = clink.get_env('VIRTUAL_ENV')
    if virtual_env == nil then
        return nil
    end

    local activate_bat = io.open(virtual_env..'\\Scripts\\activate.bat', 'r')
    if activate_bat == nil then
        return nil
    end
    local content = activate_bat:read('*a')
    activate_bat:close()

    return content:match('set "PROMPT=%((.+)%) %%PROMPT%%"')
end

local function set_prompt_filter()
    local env = find_virtualenv_prompt()
    clink.prompt.value = "format the prompt here..."
end
```

I've also identified the issue with using Boolean or integer templates on Python 2.x. The template is not converted to floating point values. In line 144 of  scikit-image/skimage/feature/template.py
````
    nom = xcorr - image_window_sum * (template.sum() / template_volume)
```` 
the template values are summed and divided by volume. For boolean and integer arrays the sum will be an integer, the template volume is also an integer. In python 2.x this results in integer division, while in python 3.x all division are converted to floating point.  Using:
````
from __future__ import division
````
fixed it.

Problem is:
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = ['django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend']

the default "ModelBackend" authentication backend does not allow users with is_active = False to log in.

so if you want to login with is_active= False, try
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = ['django.contrib.auth.backends.AllowAllUsersModelBackend']

checkout : https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/contrib/auth/#django.contrib.auth.models.User.is_active

for more detail.

Running nose from within SCons requires that the `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` is included in the executable line (which is what LSST does when running tests) because the Scons environment will not forward through the `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` to subproceses.

Starting from v1.4 Patroni exposes information about current timeline.
If the postgres is running as a master, it is possible to query timeline as:
`SELECT ('x' || SUBSTR(pg_xlogfile_name(pg_current_xlog_location()), 1, 8))::bit(32)::int`

But for the replicas the only way to fetch information about current timeline - open a replication connection and get it from there.
Obviously pg_hba.conf by default (until postgres 10) doesn't allow replication connection from the localhost and you solved the problem by adding the rule to pg_hba.

I made this kubernetes example as a proof of concept while was working on implementing kubernetes support (all production clusters we run with Spilo). After that I've never touch it, except may be changing it to postgres 10 recently: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/tree/feature/demo/kubernetes, but it lives in a feature branch and not merged yet. Actually switching to postgres 10 also helps, because initdb adds following lines to the pg_hba.conf by default:
```
host    replication     all             127.0.0.1/32            md5
host    replication     all             ::1/128                 md5
```

We implemented support for reading Patroni configuration from the environment variable (PATRONI_CONFIGURATION at present). It's trivially to emulate the use-case above by doing

`PATRONI_CONFIGUARION="... yaml configuration" patroni.py`

Since JSON is a subset of YAML, our existing code should handle a JSON configuration as well.

> namely the hosts members are not attempted to be resolved via DNS,

The only reason to resolve host into multiple ip addresses was to increase chances to find accessible member of etcd cluster from the first attempt (for loop over ip addresses)

> therefore, they are assumed to be IP addresses

No, it could be anything, host or IP. If you have more than one value specified in the `hosts` your chances already quite high, doesn't really pay of to resolve it separately.

Host and hosts are used only for initial discovery. As soon as request `GET hostN:port/v2/machines` succeed, Patroni will use the list of members returned by that request.

So I'm not surprised we have a problem here, but the issue seems to boil down to the fact that no two OAuth2 providers seem to agree on how they want auth passed to them. If you look through the issues on this repo, there is stunning inconsistency across the lot of them as to whether they accept Basic, body-only, both, or either but only one. It's simply not possible to automatically get this right for all implementations.

Generally speaking, then, the MUST NOT being referred to in this case is not actually being enforced across most OAuth implementations. For that reason, I'm inclined to want to keep the current behaviour at this time.

However, you can force us to not send the Basic auth header by setting `auth=False` when you call `fetch_token`. That should resolve your issue.

I'm not sure what you're asking, but the crux of the issue is this:

1. You start `endpointscfg.py`.
2. `endpointscfg.py` import `oauth2client`. At this point, `sys.path` only contains the App Engine SDK, so the App Engine SDK's version of `oauth2client<2.0` is loaded into `sys.path`. 
3. `endpointscfg.py` tries to import your code.
4. Your code imports `appengine_config.py` which does path manipulation.
5. Your code tries to import `gcloud`, which in turn tries to import `oauth2client`.
6. Because `endpointscfg.py` had previously import `oauth2client`, it's already in `sys.modules` so not search is done and the newer, vendored version isn't used.
7. Because `gcloud` expects `oauth2client>2.0` but gets `oauth2client<2.0` it explodes.

Adding the trickery to unload `oauth2client` in `appengine_config.py` allows things to work as expected.

`google.api_core.operation.Operation._set_result_from_operation` is raising this error because its `self._operation.done` flag is already true, but neither `error` nor `response` is set.  This is a bug in the backend:  the [API docs for `Operation`](https://cloud.google.com/speech-to-text/docs/reference/rpc/google.longrunning#google.longrunning.Operation) state:

> Union field result. The operation result, which can be either an error or a valid response. If done == false, neither error nor response is set. If done == true, exactly one of error or response is set. result can be only one of the following:
> error | Status                                        The error result of the operation in case of failure or cancellation.
>
> response | Any                                        The normal response of the operation in case of  success. If the original method returns no data on success, such as Delete, the response is google.protobuf.Empty. If the original method is standard Get/Create/Update, the response should be the resource. For other methods, the response should have the type XxxResponse, where Xxx is the original method name. For example, if the original method name is TakeSnapshot(), the inferred response type is TakeSnapshotResponse.

I have experienced a similar problem.  There are a couple of things going on.

First, apache_beam is using the 'extra_requires' feature of setup/pip to pull in the GCP_REQUIREMENTS.  Unfortunately, pip does not report constraint violations for 'extra_requires' packages.  It's easy to end up with a newer version of google-cloud-pubsub (e.g., 0.37.1) with no warning about the constraint violation.

The google-cloud-pubsub package at 0.26.0 needed proto-google-cloud-datastore-v1, but pubsub 0.37.x includes the protobuf definitions.  So, if you have 0.37 and the ancient proto-google-cloud-datastore-v1, python complains about multiple definitions of the protos.

I got around it by using the newer pubsub and `pip uninstall proto-google-cloud-datastore-v1`

On Fri, May 26, 2017 at 10:25 AM Charles Duffy <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> chowning is effectively done automatically by the filesystem -- you create
> something with UID x, it's stored with UID y, because *everything* on the
> filesystem is always, unconditionally, chowned to UID y (with no user,
> including root, able to prevent or override same). Hence, if you just did a
> mkdir with 0700 permissions, you can't *even as an immediately following
> syscall* cd into that directory you created, make a new file, there, etc.
>
> Following along after-the-fact is thus much too late -- our attempt to
> store files in a directory wal-e just created will have already failed by
> the time we get there. Unless you mean to hook that directly into wal-e's
> makedirs() operations or similar, but that seems like overkill -- I'd
> rather just put ?perms= on the file:// url to let the user override the
> 0700 default.

Hello aginpatrick,

The message is _from_ unixODBC, which is actually an external C library.  That library is trying to load the FreeTDS library, but can't find it.  Thus, deep in the bowels of C, you're not going to get nice tracebacks.

Virtualenv is not a full container -- it just wraps the python libraries.  If a python library has an external, system dependency, then virtualenv doesn't actually manage that.  If you need a fully containerized instance you might need a new machine, or a new virtual machine, or possibly do some very custom chroot jailing.

To resolve the FreeTDS dependency on ubuntu, install the library with:

    sudo apt-get install freetds-dev 

You'll still have to configure your other freetds and unixodbc library config files which are at:
    /etc/odbcinst.ini
    /etc/odbc.ini /etc/
    /etc/freetds/freetds.conf


Closing as not a bug, but feel free to comment more or reopen if you have more evidence that this is still a django-pyodbc issue.

> I'm curious where cff was indicated

the OP typed `fontmake -u NotoSansLao-Bold.ufo`, so fontmake by default tries to compile both OTF and TTF, in that order.

However, the AssertionError in the traceback above was *not* caused by fontmake trying to create a CFF-OTF from a UFO containing TrueType outlines (this is not supported yet, depends on https://github.com/googlei18n/cu2qu/pull/29 being landed first).
The problem is that booleanOperations can't remove overlaps from contours with TrueType outlines ("qcurve"), and fontmake by default attempts to remove the overlaps, unless told otherwise with `--keep-overlaps` option.

I haven't checked the source files, but if they contain quadratic contours, it's likely that these were generated by decompiling binary TTF rather than drawn that way, and hence they may already have overlaps removed. Passing options `--keep-overlaps -o ttf` should complete successfully.

`/deis/stateless-platform/version` isn't an etcd key that's used anywhere.  The `get_image` utility only takes `/deis/stateless-platform/version` into account.  So for the benefit of anyone who encounters this same problem, what happened would have been this... that your upgraded `deisctl` used v1.10.0 units to start v1.9.1 components.  There are some incompatibilities between the newer builder unit and the older builder image.

Essentially our AngularJS SPA only ever make requests to our newest API, it never makes direct calls to our legacy API. However the legacy API endpoint gets exposed to the SPA when enforceHTTPS is on due to what I believe is the Deis router doing aa 301 redirect.

On the question of handling Unicode, you might want to read Michael Kleehammer's thoughts on this.  He recently did a fairly major update of pyodbc to handle all the different RDBMS's and drivers, adding "encoding" parameters to the `connect` function. Here's some links:
http://mkleehammer.github.io/pyodbc/unicode.html
https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Connecting-to-MySQL
https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Connecting-to-PostgreSQL

https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/blob/09072a08b68a2a2b06cb8bb638341c7cc62f1511/src/cursor.cpp#L594-L597

http://www-01.ibm.com/support/docview.wss?uid=swg21674147

which eventually takes you here:

I'm not happy right now with the split between `subdir` (https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.2.13/conda/base/constants.py#L73-L80) and `Platform` + `Arch` (https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.2.13/conda/base/constants.py#L18-L45) right now.  Yes also relevant is https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.2.13/conda/base/context.py#L155-L173.

But that context object is incredibly configurable.  If your using conda programmatically, either in-process or shelling out to it, I think the easiest way is to use environment variables.  Any of the growing list of config parameters (https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.2.13/conda/base/context.py#L155-L173) can be set with an environment variable prepending `CONDA_` and then the rest of the parameter in all caps with underscores.

Several config parameters have aliases.  The `context.always_yes` parameter is a good example.  It is aliased to `yes`.  So setting `CONDA_ALWAYS_YES` and `CONDA_YES` with have the equivalent result.

Configuration is also merged from quite a few sources.  We first scan `[SEARCH_PATH](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.2.13/conda/base/context.py#L91)` for files that look like condarc, .yml, and .yaml.  Then we look at environment variables.  Then we look at CLI flags from argparse.  Parameters set later in that chain override parameters set earlier.  At least for the "primitive" parameter type.

Sequence parameter types, like `channel`, are a little trickier.  The lists are merged.  It's definitely caused some confusion.  I'm way behind on updating the docs.  But if it causes you problems let me know and I'll help with solutions.

@gnestor in general, PRs are against master unless they only affect a backport branch. If a PR affects master and should be backported, the general flow is:

0. mark PR with milestone for the next backport release (4.3)
1. merge into master
2. backport to 4.x
3. push updated 4.x branch

Backports can be done in a variety of ways, but we have [a script](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/tools/backport_pr.py) for automating the common process of:

1. download [the patch](https://patch-diff.githubusercontent.com/raw/jupyter/notebook/pull/1645.patch)
2. checkout the 4.x branch
3. apply the patch
4. make a commit

which works for simple cases, at leats.

In this case, it would be:

    python /path/to/ipython-repo/tools/backport_pr.py jupyter/notebook 4.x 1645

After master and backport branches have diverged, the script may not work and backporting the patch by hand may be necessary. If you want to be conservative after manually backporting a patch, a PR against the backport branch would be appropriate.

I'm currently wrapping up a validation refactoring/cleanup PR and would like to throw this feature in.

In addition to @jvantuyl's analysis above, here's something to consider: model-level validators are currently called even if the field's value is `None`. I'd like to stop doing this for two reasons:

  * Consistency with type-level validators, which are never called when the value is missing or `None`
  * Calling a validator with `None` means the same ought to happen with an undefined value too. In practice, this would mean supplying the raw `Undefined` object as the value. But that conflicts with the principle that applications should never have to deal with the `Undefined` placeholders unless they want to.

The only situation in which a validator needs to check for `None` is if it implements some kind of conditional `required` constraint. Typically such a constraint would depend on the values of several fields and would therefore belong in a global validator as proposed in this issue, not in a field-specific validator.

Since the global validator functions would receive the entire dataset as a dictionary argument, undefined values would pose no problem, as they would simply be absent.

I just saw your original tweet by the way, apologies for being terrible at keeping track of Twitter.

I picked up most of my VCS habits from Facebook, and in that school of thought yours would be a clear use case for a One Repo to Rule Them All.

> Most teams just siloed themselves rather than dealing with the hassle of having to have all common cause in a single repository.

I'm curious to hear a little more about the hassles here. Are you talking more about the bloat/hassle of trying to make one project satisfy all of your company's different needs? I think you can make good use of the Monster Repo without going that far, by letting mostly-unrelated projects live in their own subdirectories and just sharing common dependencies as appropriate. Separate Makefiles and Visual Studio solutions and all that stuff, but one shared commit history.

In the Monster, your git logs contain actual commits. Also changes across projects can be atomic, and they don't automatically become unresolvable merge conflicts. That's huge.

The potential downside (depending on who you ask) is that changes in a dependency project immediately affects all the callers. This can make it harder to make work on your libraries without breaking everyone else. However, if you're updating dependencies every day, you already have this problem. Unifying the repos will actually make your life easier, because developers will have a chance at telling what callers they broke immediately, instead of waiting for the calling projects to sync and finding out the next day. You no longer have to do anything special to test changes in the caller and the library at the same time.

Not directly related but may help if you are just adding a couple of links to the default list. The following snippet modifies the "Fulfilment" drop down to add the shipping controls that are disabled by default.

Adding the following to your settings (settings.py) will append the new link at the end of the fulfilment link. 

```
# Loop through the dashboard config and append new children
for navigation in OSCAR_DASHBOARD_NAVIGATION:
    if(navigation.get('label') == _('Fulfilment')):
        navigation.get('children').append(
            # The shipping method dashboard is disabled by default as it might
            # be confusing. Weight-based shipping methods aren't hooked into
            # the shipping repository by default (as it would make
            # customising the repository slightly more difficult).
            {
                'label': _('Shipping charges'),
                'url_name': 'dashboard:shipping-method-list',
            },
        ) 
```

The solution is a little hacky but I thought it was better than forking the entire list, now I only need to care about the word Fulfilment changing. You could add a test to test for this when updating if necessary.

Okay, by my re-scan everyone who has chimed in has attained success; so I'm going to give a quick closing summary and close the issue:

# Resolution Summary

The `dotnet` utility (or, rather, the cryptographic subsystems within the framework libraries) requires libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib (and libssl.1.0.0.dylib) to be present in a standard rpath location, e.g. `/usr/local/lib/`.

### Homebrew users using default paths

```
$ brew link --force openssl
```

The primary effect of this command is to create a symlink from /usr/local/lib/libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib to /usr/local/Cellar/openssl/\<version\>/lib/libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib (and similalry for libssl.1.0.0.dylib).  It also links the openssl utility into /usr/local/bin, and creates other symlinks useful for building new libraries/applications against openssl.

### Other users (MacPorts, DIY, non-standard paths from Homebrew)

You need to create versioned dylib symlinks from a standard library directory to wherever your installed copies are.  For example:

```
ln -s /opt/local/lib/libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib /usr/local/lib/libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib
ln -s /opt/local/lib/libssl.1.0.0.dylib /usr/local/lib/libssl.1.0.0.dylib
```

> I just noticed that these two files are different in the repository?

Yes. So the file `/letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto` is the latest, most bleeding edge version of the script. #2569 should land soon, after which `/letsencrypt-auto` will be version 0.4.1 and using this version of the script will work for you. In the meantime, you can use the script available [here](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/v0.4.1/letsencrypt-auto) which is what `letsencrypt-auto` is downloading when it upgrades to 0.4.1.

In particular, I renamed several files in /etc/letsencrypt because I didn't like the -0001 suffix which LE had added to one of my domain names. Although I took care of any symlinks that needed to be updated, I missed the _content_ of the renewal config file which included several pointers to a renamed directory which were now broken. LE is working again since I fixed that file.

Fixing this problem at the root is a cantfix as there is no way of patching HID operating units (at least none that I know of).

However, I think that this kind of error could be handled more gracefully (e. g. by adding an exception handler in the right place).

The "usual" approach is to buffer everything locally and then provide the client with what it requires; this is typical of async implementations, i.e. Twisted in Python.

There's data, any data? just read it and cache it locally always, as a first step:

```
self._buffer += data
```

then, go on processing read_until(), if the terminator is in the current buffer you just pick the data in the buffer up to that, you remove it from the buffer, and you return it to the caller. If a timeout expires, the data is still in the buffer and can be read by successive client calls; nothing gets lost.

Anyway, I wasn't advocating changing how pyserial works now, as it would break current clients. Maybe a new class or utility function that "wraps" serial objects would be fine. 

But, forget it. Probably your idea of pyserial is of a much lower level tool than I'd expect; I was sincerely puzzled by read_until(), because it looked like an "higher level" method, while it really wasn't.

No, that is not correct.

If you supply a feature matrix, the i-th column of that matrix has a latent representation in the i-th row of the embeddings.

If you do not supply a feature matrix, an (num_items x num_items) identity feature matrix is generated for you.

If you'd like to obtain the behaviour your describe, you need to supply a feature matrix of the appropriate structure.

One thing you could do is rank items by a weighed average of the user-item recommendation score and the item-item similarity score.

If you do that, you may want to make sure to normalize the user-item recommendation scores appropriately, for example by transforming them into percentiles. User-item scores out of the LightFM model do not have a guaranteed range or scale (as that is irrelevant for ranking items for any particular user).

```
import functools, time, os, ipyparallel
import sacred


def partial_but_seed(func, **kwargs):
    def wrapped_func(seed):
        return func(seed=seed, **kwargs)
    return wrapped_func

def create_main_map(seeds, mapper=map, **kwargs):
    def real_wrapper(func):
        @functools.wraps(func)

        def wrapped_func(*cannot_use_args, **cannot_use_kwargs):
            tic = time.time()

            rc = mapper(partial_but_seed(func, **kwargs), seeds)

            for i,value in enumerate(rc):
                print ("%4i/%i tasks finished after %4.1f s" % (i, len(seeds), time.time()-tic))
            
            return list(rc)
        return wrapped_func
    return real_wrapper


if __name__ == '__main__':
    def func(a,b,seed):
        print (a,b,seed)
        return (a,b,seed)

    # test 1: plain map
    map(partial_but_seed(func,a=3,b=4), xrange(10))

    # test 2: parallel map
    c = ipyparallel.Client()
    v = c.load_balanced_view()
    rc = v.map(partial_but_seed(func, a=5,b=6), xrange(10))
    print(rc.get())

    #test 3: only ex
    main = create_main_map(xrange(10), a=7, b=8)(func)

    obs = sacred.observers.MongoObserver.create(
         url='localhost:' + os.environ.get('MONGOPORT', 27017),
         db_name='test_min_example', prefix='test_min_example')

    ex = sacred.Experiment('test_min_example')
    ex.observers.append(obs)

    ex.main(main)
    print(vars(ex.run()))

    # test 4: ex parallel
    main_par = create_main_map(xrange(10), mapper=v.map, a=9, b=10)(func)
    ex.main(main_par)
    print(vars(ex.run()))
```

This is known and expected. Currently these operations are bit-blasted (eagerly) and they produce huge SAT problems, in the case of double precision fp.rem, my desktop runs out of memory during bit-blasting, before it does any solving. There is a time/memory trade-off to be considered, but so far I haven't seen any meaningful practical application of this operation, so I'm reluctant to invest effort on turning memory-outs into time-outs which ultimately doesn't help anybody.

(Aside: This is also the implicit reason why many CPUs (including Intel x86, x64), don't offer a full fp.rem instruction; the circuit would be too large. They only offer two versions of partial remainder operations, FPREM and FPREM1, which can be used to get precise results through iteration, i.e., they are also trading time for memory.)

I posted an implementation [on our mailing list some time ago](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/lasagne-users/KB4SmoODtkg/FKCaR5wlCwAJ):
```python
class Deconv2DLayer(lasagne.layers.Layer):

    def __init__(self, incoming, num_filters, filter_size, stride=1, pad=0,
            nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify, **kwargs):
        super(Deconv2DLayer, self).__init__(incoming, **kwargs)
        self.num_filters = num_filters
        self.filter_size = lasagne.utils.as_tuple(filter_size, 2, int)
        self.stride = lasagne.utils.as_tuple(stride, 2, int)
        self.pad = lasagne.utils.as_tuple(pad, 2, int)
        self.W = self.add_param(lasagne.init.Orthogonal(),
                (self.input_shape[1], num_filters) + self.filter_size,
                name='W')
        self.b = self.add_param(lasagne.init.Constant(0),
                (num_filters,),
                name='b')
        if nonlinearity is None:
            nonlinearity = lasagne.nonlinearities.identity
        self.nonlinearity = nonlinearity

    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shape):
        shape = tuple(i*s - 2*p + f - 1
                for i, s, p, f in zip(input_shape[2:],
                                      self.stride,
                                      self.pad,
                                      self.filter_size))
        return (input_shape[0], self.num_filters) + shape

    def get_output_for(self, input, **kwargs):
        op = T.nnet.abstract_conv.AbstractConv2d_gradInputs(
            imshp=self.output_shape,
            kshp=(self.input_shape[1], self.num_filters) + self.filter_size,
            subsample=self.stride, border_mode=self.pad)
        conved = op(self.W, input, self.output_shape[2:])
        if self.b is not None:
            conved += self.b.dimshuffle('x', 0, 'x', 'x')
        return self.nonlinearity(conved)
```
It doesn't support string shortcuts for the `pad` parameter yet and lacks docstrings and tests, but should provide a good basis.

I think this from this part of the error message: 

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/circleci/project/src/pynwb/form/build/map.py", line 675, in construct
    obj = cls(**kwargs)
  File "/home/circleci/project/src/pynwb/form/utils.py", line 346, in func_call
    raise_from(TypeError(msg), None)
  File "<string>", line 3, in raise_from
TypeError: incorrect type for 'emission_lambda' (got 'str', expected 'float')
```

This tells me wrong type is getting passed into the constructor.  In the test suite, there are two ways the constructor gets called: 1) when you explicitly call it in the tests and 2) when the middle layer builds the objects that get read in. We know the the first way shouldn't raise this error, since we can look at the code. So, it must be the second way. When the *emission_lamba* gets read in, it's string. You gave it a float, so at some point along the way, it got converted to string, either before or after it was written. 

It happens before it's written. I know this because I can look at the results of `test_build`.

```
...
-               'emission_lambda': {'attributes': {}, 'data': '500.0'},
?                                                             -     -

+               'emission_lambda': {'attributes': {}, 'data': 500.0},
...
```

The handwritten Builder had a float (you just changed those), but the Builder produced by the middle layer had strings. So, the middle layer converted the floats to strings. 

Why would such a thing happen? Because the specification says *emission_lambda* should be `text`, it converts it from a float to string. The code that does this is [here](https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/blob/dev/src/pynwb/form/build/map.py#L380)

Hope that helps.

Hi Riyad,

The short answer is, angr is not good enough :-)

The long answer is, that we never put enough effort to make angr's CFG as complete as possible. We did a bit experiment (on our upcoming angr release), which shows that even after quite some improvement to angr's CFG generation algorithm, our CFG coverage is only equal to or better than IDA Pro's for 60% of all test cases we have. We are looking into it now, and I believe it will be greatly improved with a) more implementation bugs fixed, and b) the upcoming object-sensitivity analysis feature.

If you'd like to debug angr and see why it's slow, you would have to look into `angr/analyses/cfg.py` and manually set some hooks there. Turn on logging output for `CFG` class by using `logging.getLogger('angr.analyses.cfg').setLevel(logging.DEBUG)`, which should give you some idea of what CFG is doing.

The code of CFG class right now is a bit messy (in the sense of not grouping related methods together, and unnecessarily using unnamed tuples instead of proper classes), which will be reorganized in the next two releases. Sorry if you want to read the code before that :P

yes, you can do that for `pytraj.pca`.

In general you can use either

```python
pt.radgyr(traj[1000:7000:10])

# traj[1000:7000:10] is similar to slicing a Python list
```

or

```python
# use iterload for lazy loading
traj = pt.iterload(fn, tn)
pt.radgyr(traj(1000, 7000, 10))
# this does not apply for pytraj.pca
```
This is similiar to `range(1000, 7000, 10)` in python 3 (lazy evaluation): Only a single snapshot would be loaded to memory at a time. This is designed for streaming calculation, good for tens of GB or TB data.

A stupid yet effecticve workaround is to restart tensorboard server every `t` seconds:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
trap ctrl_c INT
function ctrl_c() {
        echo 
	echo "Ctrl-C by user"
	kill -9 $pid
	exit
}
args=$@
while true
do
	eval "tensorboard $args > /dev/null 2>&1 &"
	pid=$!
	echo "tensorboard running on $pid"
	sleep 20
	kill -9 $pid
done
```

@Nopik, thank you for sharing your findings with such detail!

I think everything you have described is as designed and expected.

In particular, the route entries maintain both path cost and link cost separately.  Route cost is the best path to a given router ID that is advertised by a neighboring router.  In your 'long routes' example above, `8c00` -> `bc00` is `8` because it represents the path `8c00` -> `dc00` -> `8c00`  -> `bc00`.  Yes, the path is a loop, but the routing protocol has no way to determine that on its own.  For the case of `dc00` -> `8c00`, the path cost is 0 because there is no multihop path that exists (even with loops).

 Link cost is the cost to reach the neighbor directly.  Of course, the link cost itself is `4` and when forwarding a packet, the next hop lookup will compare communicating with the neighbor directly or route via a different neighbor.

Having the route entries separate link and path costs gives an opportunity to continue forwarding messages even if a direct link no longer exists.

You can find additional detail in #1187.

As for the link cost itself, only seeing value `4` is expected.  Your CLI output shows that links are configured to simulate -90 dBm RSSI.  The posix platform has sensitivity level hard-coded at -100 dBm.  As a result, the link margins are 10 dB, which corresponds to link quality `1` and link cost `4`, as defined in Thread 1.1.1 Section 5.9.4.

Hope that helps.

And 'No' is the answer to your question regarding how many ways there are for a library to call back to an unknown module.  Depending on how tight resources are you could define a function that the 'other' module must implement.  This eliminates the registration logic and allows for a direct function call without pointers. That solution would offer the most efficient mechanism for the single mode systems and only require extra code for the multi-mode solution.

    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 certbot […]

Is the `organizationId` available in the response? At a quick glance, I don't see that in the docs. Aside from that, `simple_salesforce.login.SalesforceLogin` completely abstracts the response. Now, that said, you *could* hack around to get what you want with a `requests.Session`, something to the effect of:

```
response_data = None
def response_hook(resp):
    global response_data
    response_data = resp.content

sess = requests.Session()
sess.hooks['response'] = response_hook
sfdc = simple_salesforce.Salesforce(session=sess, ...)
```

The hook will be called by `requests` innards and you can grab the data that way (the `Session` instance passed into `Salesforce` is passed into the login attempt.

> we should ensure that all events received

Just as clarification, these are not *incoming* events from the Matrix service, [but Raiden events passed to start](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/7684f387e01c6482616bff5ce3d12573685e9ae6/raiden/raiden_service.py#L269)

>  are processed synchronously in the matrix start before returning.

Also, we don't need to process the events in the sense "push the data to the chat rooms", we only need to make sure the `MatrixTransport` instance is properly initialized to avoid race conditions where a user action is handled before the events from the queue (assuming this is even possible).

> This is required as we need to make sure that the API doesn't get called while the matrix is still syncing messages

Also, not just the Matrix transport, but any transport must be fully initialized once `RaidenService.start` is done.

I found the culprit, the notebook code had this marvel:

```
df = train[columns]
df = test[columns]
```

so that the data was missed, and resulting in categories with just `NaN`s in them.

Deleting the 2nd line fixes things up, but I have a question here - what if some values of the categories appear only in the test set and not in the train set? I think it's much safer to combine the two and ensure we get them all. i.e.:

`df = train[columns].append(test[columns])`

I tested that the combination of the two works.

Version [0.0.14](https://github.com/mymarilyn/clickhouse-driver/compare/0.0.13...0.0.14) with this feature was released.

This only effects the `httptoolstransport`. The easiest fix is to set `shutdown_wait_period`, and `shutdown_grace_period` to 0. Shutdown will then take 0 seconds.

shutdown_wait_period is the time to wait after getting a shutdown signal before actually shutting down.
shutdown_grace_period is the time to wait for connections to be empty before shutting down. 
(both in seconds)

I need to work on a better fix for this, but its looking a little bigger than I hopped. So those should help for now.
An even better fix, is that setting the app to `debug` should eliminate all graceful shutdowns. Also, the defaults for these values should probably prefer local dev, and allow prod to set them higher. 



The history for this is that in kubernetes, there is a race condition where your service gets a shutdown before its taken out of the load balancer. These setting allow you to tweek things, so that your service gets shutdown after connections actually stop, thus preventing service disruption during a rolling deploy. However, I have found that this can be solved in other ways, so I need to experiment removing these values now.
